next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The first time was in his room, because her room had a window that faced the floodlit outdoor track and neither of them wanted to see it.

His diagnostic display was on standby — a dim green pulse on the wall, the numbers minimized to a single status bar. Heart rate, bridge latency, graft integration. All normal. He'd stopped checking it every hour about a week ago, which probably counted as progress.

Lena sat at the head of the bed with her knees drawn up and her phone facedown on the mattress. She'd come over after the pool. Her hair was still damp, the chlorine smell so constant on her that he'd stopped noticing it except when she was close. They'd been talking about nothing — a movie someone had put on in the common room, whether the cafeteria coffee was getting worse or they were all just adjusting to the altitude — and the conversation had run down the way conversations do when neither person wants to leave but neither has a reason to stay that they're ready to name.

He was sitting at the other end of the bed. His left wrist was on his knee, the ache doing its low-grade thing, the bone reminding him it had been broken and reassembled on a schedule the rest of his body hadn't agreed to. The scar tissue on his cheek had smoothed to a pink shine. Six weeks ago he'd been bleeding on the track with his legs still twitching. Now he was sitting on a narrow institutional bed with a woman who smelled like chlorine, and the distance between those two states had no name he could find.

"You're staring at your wrist," Lena said.

"I'm not staring."

"You're lookin...
open_hooks_to_carry: Margot experiences Lena's desync from inside the feed—personality thinning to nothing, replaced by pure system optimization
Margot pushes Lena's heart rate into the red zone during the desync
Lena surfaces unable to remember the last two laps
Margot lies when Lena asks what happened in the water

payoffs_to_land.description: The desync is far worse than anyone has reported—Lena loses entire segments of conscious experience

prev_tail_excerpt:
...they do."

She picked up her cup and walked toward the door. Stopped. She didn't turn around, but she wasn't leaving either. She was standing in the doorway the way someone stands when they've said what they came to say and now there's one more thing that wasn't part of the plan.

"If the alternative approach you're developing shows clinical promise," she said, "I'd want to see the data. Before it goes anywhere near a live system. That's not a request from the medical director. That's a request from a physician."

"Understood."

"Good night, Dr. Qadir."

"Night."

The door closed. The calibration bay returned to its hum, its fluorescent buzz, its blue-white light on the desk where Yusuf sat. His coffee was cold. His patch was sixty percent finished and the forty percent that remained was the part that could kill someone if he got it wrong. The virus code on his personal archive contained structural signatures that pointed, suggestively, inconclusively, damningly, toward the company that built the systems the virus was destroying and that had arrived within a week with a solution that happened to cost exactly what they'd been trying to buy for two years.

He couldn't prove it. He couldn't share it yet. He couldn't stop the shape of it from sitting in his head, solid and ugly, while the processing equipment hummed and the mountains outside the window emerged slowly from the dark as the sky began, just barely, to lighten.

He saved his work. He closed his displays. He sat in the bay for a while longer, in the hum, in the dark that wasn't quite dark anymore, and did not sleep.
state_deltas_required: Margot's addiction exploits Lena's vulnerability at the worst possible moment
Coach-athlete trust revealed as exploitative; Lena's desync severity concealed to protect Margot's access
